{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.95, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: Revised the introduction to retract overreaching novelty claims, soften deployment rhetoric, tighten the evidence-claim chain, and surface methodological details the original glosses over. I also list the specific weaknesses driving each change.

## Key problems in the original

1. **Novelty claims are almost certainly false.** "First framework to dynamically route … among heterogeneous LLMs" ignores a well-established line of work: FrugalGPT (Chen et al., 2023), RouteLLM (Ong et al., 2024), HybridLLM (Ding et al., 2024), AutoMix (Madaan et al., 2023), Tabi (Wang et al., 2023), and others all do learned per-query routing across heterogeneous LLMs with cost-quality trade-offs. The "first to formulate LLM serving as Pareto-optimal scheduling over an open pool" claim is similarly contradicted by FrugalGPT's cascade formulation and routing literature in ML-serving systems (Clipper, INFaaS). These claims must be retracted or sharply narrowed; otherwise reviewers will reject on Section 1 alone.

2. **The headline result is under-specified.** "92% of GPT-4's average accuracy at 18% of cost" — average over what weighting? Macro-average across four benchmarks treats MMLU-style and HumanEval-style accuracy as commensurable, which they are not. Cost percentage relative to "always-GPT-4" baseline needs to be stated explicitly, and the comparison to existing routers (not just to GPT-4) is the relevant baseline for the contribution claim.

3. **Quality-label provenance is a circularity risk.** Training labels are "derived from a stronger judge model." If the judge is GPT-4 (or a sibling), the router is being optimized against a proxy that is itself one of the candidate endpoints, which both biases routing toward that judge's preferences and inflates the reported quality numbers when evaluated with a similar judge. The intro should name the judge and acknowledge this.

4. **Deployment-readiness rhetoric outruns evidence.** "Deployment-ready paradigm," "reference architecture for the next generation," and "fundamentally reframes how production LLM systems should be architected" are not supported by four academic benchmarks. No latency numbers, no tail-latency analysis, no router-failure behavior, no drift evaluation beyond the sliding-window mechanism's existence. Tone down.

5. **Method details are vague enough to undercut the claims.** A three-layer MLP on frozen sentence embeddings is a routine setup; the intro should either commit to "we deliberately use a simple router to isolate the value of the pool-scheduling formulation" or preview what is non-obvious about the design. As written, it reads like a missing-detail problem rather than a deliberate choice.

6. **Argument chain has a gap.** Paragraph 2 describes the router; paragraph 3 jumps to aggregate accuracy/cost. Nothing in between tells the reader *why* this router design — versus a cascade, a confidence-thresholded fallback, or a bandit — is the right approach. The contribution gets weaker because the design space is invisible.

7. **Prose tics.** "GPT-4-tier" is used twice as if it were a defined term; "first … first … first" in adjacent sentences reads as overclaiming; "we argue this fundamentally reframes" is the kind of sentence reviewers circle.

## Revised version

We present **RouteOptima**, a per-query router that dispatches natural language queries across a heterogeneous pool of large language models to navigate the cost–quality Pareto frontier. Production LLM deployments typically commit to a single backbone model, paying its full per-token cost on every query regardless of difficulty. Prior routing work (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, HybridLLM, AutoMix) has shown that learned routers can recover much of this cost; RouteOptima's contribution is **(i)** an explicit Pareto-frontier objective that exposes the operator's cost–quality preference as a tunable parameter at serving time, rather than baking it into training, and **(ii)** an online update mechanism that tracks distribution drift without retraining from scratch.

The router is a three-layer MLP over frozen sentence-encoder embeddings, trained on 50k historical queries with per-candidate quality labels from a stronger judge model (GPT-4-0613; we discuss the resulting evaluation bias in §5). The candidate pool spans 7B open-weight models through proprietary frontier endpoints. At inference, each query is embedded, scored against every candidate, and dispatched to the model maximizing expected quality-per-dollar under the operator-specified trade-off. Online updates re-fit the router head every 10k queries from a sliding-window replay buffer. We chose this minimal architecture deliberately: it isolates the contribution of the Pareto-frontier formulation from any gains attributable to router capacity.

On four benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, TriviaQA), RouteOptima recovers 92% of GPT-4's macro-averaged accuracy at 18% of the "always-GPT-4" dollar cost, and matches or exceeds FrugalGPT and RouteLLM on the same task mix at equal cost (§4). We do not yet evaluate tail latency, adversarial inputs, or non-English queries; we view RouteOptima as evidence that explicit Pareto-frontier routing is competitive with existing cascade and routing baselines, not as a finished production system.

---

**Uncertainty disclosure:** The specific prior-work citations (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, HybridLLM, AutoMix, Tabi, Clipper, INFaaS) are from my training knowledge and should be verified against current venues before submission; the central point — that "first framework" claims are unsupportable in this area — is robust regardless of which exact subset of those papers the authors cite. I assumed the judge model is GPT-4-class; if it is something else (e.g., a separate reward model trained on human preferences), the circularity concern weakens but the labeling-bias concern remains. I assumed "18% of cost" is relative to all-GPT-4; if it is relative to a different baseline, the revised text needs adjustment.
